blurb: Grown for show, not the spice rack: a low aromatic mound that erupts in a haze of dark purple bracts and rose-pink flowers from midsummer into fall, swarming with bees and butterflies when little else is blooming.
water: Low; xeric once established — needs sharp drainage
spread: Clumping woody-based mound; spreads slowly at the base, not aggressive
seasons: Aromatic green foliage on red-purple stems in summer; dark purple bracts and rose-pink flowers midsummer into fall; leaves flush red in autumn; dies back to a low woody base in winter
wildlife: A top late-season nectar plant — bees, native bees, honeybees and butterflies mob the long-blooming flowers into fall
deer: Aromatic mint-family foliage; reliably deer- and rabbit-resistant, rarely browsed
toxic: Non-toxic — an edible culinary-family herb, safe for people and pets
origin: Eastern Mediterranean — Turkey, Cyprus, and the Levant (Syria, Lebanon)
habitat: Dry rocky hillsides and scrubland on lean, well-drained ground

care.hardiness: Rated USDA zone 5 and often reliable in zone 4 given sharp drainage, so Boulder's 5b–6a winters are comfortable for it — 'Herrenhausen' is one of the hardier ornamental oreganos. As with lavender and the sages, wet, poorly drained soil in winter kills it far faster than cold ever does; a plant on gritty, fast-draining ground overwinters dependably here.[1][2]
care.planting: Plant in spring (Apr–May), or early fall (Sep) to let roots settle before hard frost. Set the crown at or slightly above grade on well-drained ground and mulch with gravel rather than bark to keep the base dry. It grows well in a pot of gritty mix — attractive spilling over a container edge — but potted roots are far less cold-hardy than the top, so overwinter a container in an unheated shed or sink it to the rim in the ground from fall through spring.[1][2]
care.sun: Full sun — six or more hours. In shade the mound flops open, blooms sparsely, and loses the red stem and purple-bract coloring; Colorado's intense high-altitude sun and dry air suit its Mediterranean origins perfectly.[1][2]
care.soil: Sharp drainage is the whole game. It wants lean, gritty, well-drained soil and does well on Boulder's neutral-to-alkaline ground as long as water moves through fast; amend heavy clay with coarse grit (not just compost) or plant on a slope or raised bed. Avoid highly acidic and avoid rich, wet, organically heavy soils, which rot the crown.[1][2]
care.water: Low and lean. Water weekly the first season to establish; after that it is genuinely xeric and gets by on occasional deep soaks in extended summer heat. Overwatering and winter wetness are the primary killers — err dry, and never let the crown sit in soggy soil.[1][2]
care.spacing: Space plants about 12–18 in apart so the mounds knit into a low drift while keeping airflow around each crown to discourage rot; give a single plant room for its 12–24 in spread.[1]
care.propagation: Easiest by division in spring or by softwood/semi-hardwood cuttings taken from non-flowering shoots in early summer, which root readily in a gritty mix. The species can be grown from seed, but named clones like 'Herrenhausen' will not come true from seed — divide or take cuttings to keep the dark-bract selection.[2]
care.bloom: Rose-pink two-lipped flowers rise above dark reddish-purple bracts from roughly July into September on the Front Range, one of the longest-running bloom shows of late summer. The bracts color up strongest in full sun and cooler spells and hold their tone as the flowers pass.[1][2]
care.feeding: Almost none. It performs best in lean soil; skip fertilizer, which pushes soft, floppy growth at the expense of flower color, fragrance, and winter hardiness. A thin gravel mulch is all it needs.[1][2]
care.pruning: Shear the whole mound back hard in early spring as new green growth breaks from the base — this keeps it compact and floriferous instead of splitting open and going woody. As with lavender, don't cut down into the bare leafless old wood, which is slow or unwilling to resprout; always leave some green on each stem. After the main flush fades you can shear the spent flower stems back to tidy the planting and sometimes coax a light rebloom. Don't cut it back hard in fall — leaving the top growth protects the crown through winter.[1][3]
care.maintenance: Low-care. Shear off spent flower stems once the bloom fades to keep it neat (and it may push a few more flowers). Rake deciduous leaf litter off the crown so it doesn't smother and rot the semi-evergreen base over winter. Divide every few years if the center thins.[1][3]
care.troubles: Root rot in wet, poorly drained soil is by far the main problem here — the fix is drainage, drainage, drainage, plus airflow and lean watering. There are no serious pests, though aphids and spider mites can appear in hot, dry, dusty spots; a hose blast handles them. Winter wetness at the crown, not cold, is what loses these plants on the Front Range.[1][3]

edible.card: Aromatic leaves & flowers — a mild oregano seasoning or tea
edible.summary: A genuine culinary-family herb: the aromatic leaves and flowers are edible and safe, just milder and more bitter than the oregano you'd cook with.[2][3]
edible.parts: The aromatic leaves and the tiny flowers are both usable in the kitchen — chopped fresh or dried as an oregano-type seasoning, steeped into a mild herbal tea, or scattered as an edible garnish. This is an ornamental selection grown chiefly for its purple bracts and long bloom, so the flavor is noticeably milder and a bit more bitter than the true culinary oregano (*Origanum vulgare* / Greek oregano), which stays the better choice for strong seasoning.[2][3]
edible.preparation: Harvest sprigs anytime through the season, best just before flowering when the leaves are most aromatic; strip leaves from the wiry stems and use fresh or air-dry them. Use with a light hand as you would any oregano — the flavor, while gentle, still reads as oregano — and rinse flowers before garnishing.[3]
edible.caution: No part of the plant is poisonous — this is a safe mint-family culinary herb, not a toxic ornamental. The only real caveats are culinary rather than safety: the flavor is milder and can be faintly bitter, so it underperforms true cooking oregano; and, as with all herbs, concentrated oregano essential oil is a different thing from the leaf and should not be swallowed. Harvest only from plants you haven't sprayed.[2][3]
